name: Astryx
description: A nine-component slice of Meta's Astryx design system (open source, MIT; 160+ components; explicitly guidance-over-enforcement: design opinions live in docs, components render whatever they are passed). This contract converts a subset of that prose guidance into machine-checkable governance. Components and guidance are sourced from the repository's structured *.doc.mjs files at the pinned tag.

alert-dialog.name: AlertDialog
alert-dialog.description: A modal dialog that asks the user to confirm a destructive action. Props-based: title, description, and action/cancel labels are string props (there is no trigger sub-component; visibility is controlled).
alert-dialog.whenToUse: AlertDialog asks the user to confirm a destructive or irreversible action before it happens. Use it for things like deleting content, revoking access, or discarding unsaved changes.
alert-dialog.whenNotToUse: Do not use AlertDialog for non-destructive actions; use a standard Dialog instead.

actionLabel.description: Action button label. Make it specific: 'Delete project' is better than 'OK' or 'Confirm'.

dialog.name: Dialog
dialog.description: A modal window over the page content. Takes arbitrary children; dismissal behavior is controlled by `purpose`.
dialog.whenNotToUse: Do not use Dialog for destructive confirmations; use AlertDialog.

variant.description: Dialog variant: fullscreen expands to fill the viewport.

purpose.description: Controls dismissal: 'required' disables Escape and backdrop click; 'form' disables backdrop click only.

composition.notes: Takes arbitrary children (ReactNode); surfaces express them as child nodes.

button.name: Button
button.description: Triggers an action when clicked. The accessible label is a REQUIRED string prop (rendered as visible text; used as aria-label when isIconOnly).
button.whenToUse: Use for form submissions, confirmations, or any interaction that needs a clear call to action.
button.whenNotToUse: Do not use a button for navigation; if it only takes the user to another page, use a link instead.

label.description: Accessible label. Rendered as visible text by default; used as aria-label when isIconOnly.

variant.description: Reserve primary for the single most important action in the view. Use destructive for irreversible actions.

isIconOnly.description: Icon-only rendering; the label becomes the aria-label.

tooltip.description: Tooltip text; recommended for icon-only buttons.

badge.name: Badge
badge.description: A small status or category label.
badge.whenToUse: Badge states where the user needs to notice or act. Use success/warning/error only for system status that demands attention; use color variants for category tags.
badge.whenNotToUse: Do not apply a success badge to every healthy/active item; if all rows show green badges, none of them communicate anything.

variant.description: Status variants (neutral/info/success/warning/error) for system state; color variants for category tags.

label.description: Badge text. Keep to one or two words.

card.name: Card
card.description: A container for a discrete item that could be reordered or removed independently.
card.whenNotToUse: Do not default to cards for visual grouping; a heading plus spacing often creates hierarchy without chrome.

variant.description: Background color variant: 'default' is the standard card background, 'muted' de-emphasises the card. Color variants are for categorization, not status (use Badge or a banner for status).

composition.notes: Takes arbitrary children (ReactNode); surfaces express them as child nodes.

text-input.name: TextInput
text-input.description: A single-line text input. The visible label is a REQUIRED string prop.
text-input.whenToUse: Use for short free-form text. Always provide a visible label; only hide it when surrounding context makes the purpose unambiguous.

label.description: Visible field label (required by the component API).

isLabelHidden.description: Visually hides the label (still announced to screen readers).

dropdown-menu.name: DropdownMenu
dropdown-menu.description: A menu opened from a trigger button. DATA-DRIVEN: menu entries are an `items` array prop, not child components — a deliberate Astryx idiom this contract documents as an array-typed prop.

items.description: Menu entries: [{label, ...}]. Handlers inside items are not surface-expressible; generation carries labels only.

hasChevron.description: Shows a chevron on the trigger.

table.name: Table
table.description: Tabular data. DATA-DRIVEN: rows and columns are array props (`data`, `columns`), not sub-components — the second instance of the Astryx data-props idiom.

data.description: Row records: each { cells: string[] } aligned with columns.

columns.description: Header labels for the data columns, e.g. ["Ingredient", "Amount"]. (The full Astryx TableColumn object shape is not yet contract-specified.)

type.description: Semantic text type; determines size, weight, and line-height from the theme. Headings are expressed via the display types (or Astryx's separate Heading component), never via the rendered element.

as.description: Rendered HTML element. Heading tags are not accepted; use `type` for heading-scale text.

composition.notes: Takes arbitrary children (ReactNode); surfaces express them as child nodes.

list.name: List
list.description: A vertical collection of items with consistent spacing, dividers, and optional markers.
list.whenToUse: Use it to display ordered or unordered groups of related content — including groups of selectable options. Lists imply a meaningful collection.
list.whenNotToUse: Do not use a list for a single item or for laying out unrelated content. Records with uniform fields belong in a table.

composition.notes: Takes arbitrary children (ReactNode); surfaces express items as child nodes — for a comparison, selectable-card children.

selectable-card.name: SelectableCard
selectable-card.description: A card that toggles between selected and unselected states with an accent border.
selectable-card.whenToUse: Use for plan pickers, filter chips, or option grids — one named option among alternatives, with its selected state visible.
selectable-card.whenNotToUse: Do not use for navigation; use ClickableCard for that (not in this contract).

label.description: The option's name. Every option is named.

isSelected.description: Whether this option is currently selected. Presentational: selection state lives in the shared data model and re-delivers this prop.

isDisabled.description: Whether the option can be chosen.

variant.description: Card background variant, verbatim from SelectableCard's union; color variants categorize rather than signal status.

composition.notes: Takes arbitrary children (ReactNode); an option's content — attributes, badges, actions — are child nodes.

metadata-list.name: MetadataList
metadata-list.description: Key-value pairs for object attributes, in a structured layout. DATA-DRIVEN: items are an array prop, the same Astryx data-props idiom as Table.
metadata-list.whenToUse: MetadataList displays key-value pairs for object attributes like quality, condition, and status. Use it for detail panels, settings summaries, and record information — the attributes that make options comparable.
metadata-list.whenNotToUse: Do not use for extensive form input (use a form layout) or for data without a clear key-value structure.

items.description: Attribute records, each { label, value } — e.g. { "label": "Storage", "value": "1 TB" }.
items.x-drift: upstream MetadataList is children-based (MetadataListItem); the contract carries items as data-props because sub-components carry no props under grammar-constrained generation

columns.description: Column layout for the attribute grid.

composition.notes: Upstream MetadataList takes MetadataListItem children; the contract carries items as data-props because sub-components carry no props under grammar-constrained generation — the renderer synthesizes the item children.

action.name: Action
action.description: Triggers an operation when activated. Mirrors Astryx's own 'Action' docs category.

overlay.name: Overlay
overlay.description: Renders above the page with its own focus and dismiss semantics. Mirrors Astryx's 'Overlay' docs category.

container.name: Container
container.description: Groups discrete content. Mirrors Astryx's 'Container' docs category.

content.name: Content
content.description: Static content presentation. Mirrors Astryx's 'Content' docs category.

data-input.name: Data Input
data-input.description: Collects user input. Mirrors Astryx's 'Data Input' docs category.

feedback.name: Feedback & Status
feedback.description: Communicates state or draws attention. Mirrors Astryx's 'Feedback & Status' docs category.

table-list.name: Table & List
table-list.description: Tabular and list data presentation. Mirrors Astryx's 'Table & List' docs category.

intents.name: Destructive action
intents.description: The requested UI performs an irreversible or high-consequence operation: deleting content, revoking access, discarding unsaved changes.

intents.name: Scheduling
intents.description: Surfaces that collect a time choice and confirm it.

intents.name: Structured editing
intents.description: Surfaces the user and agent edit together: a structured object rendered for co-editing, with explicit actions to change it.

intents.name: Record collection
intents.description: Surfaces that present many records of the same kind — support tickets, orders, members — for scanning and action. Records share uniform fields, so the collection renders as a table; each record carries a status the user reads at a glance, summarized with badges where attention is needed and plain text otherwise.

intents.name: Data collection
intents.description: Surfaces that ask the user for information and submit it — signup, contact, profile edits, an RSVP. The ask is stated, the fields are labeled, and the submit action is explicit: at minimum, something to fill in and a way to send it.

intents.name: Transactional review
intents.description: Surfaces that present alternatives for a consequential choice — plans, offers, bookings, products — compared on stated attributes and committed with an explicit action. Options are named, comparable, and at least two; the commitment is a labeled action.

rules.rationale: "Don't use AlertDialog for non-destructive actions; use a standard Dialog instead" — and its converse: AlertDialog "asks the user to confirm a destructive or irreversible action before it happens". A destructive flow must use the confirmation dialog, not the dismissible one — Astryx AlertDialog docs, v0.1.4

title, description, and actionLabel are required by the component API (aria-labelledby / aria-describedby wiring), and "describe what will happen in the description so the user knows the consequences before confirming". The schema does not check prop presence; this rule does — Astryx AlertDialog docs, v0.1.4

"Make the action button label specific: 'Delete project' is better than 'OK' or 'Confirm'". The forbidden values are the generic labels the guidance names (plus their nearest kin) — Astryx AlertDialog docs, v0.1.4

"Always provide a label for icon-only buttons so screen readers can announce what the button does" — and label is the component's required accessible name in every mode. The schema does not check prop presence; this rule does — Astryx Button docs, v0.1.4

"Always provide a visible label so users know what the field is for. Only hide the label when surrounding context makes the purpose unambiguous" (and then via isLabelHidden, which keeps it announced — never by omitting it) — Astryx TextInput docs, v0.1.4

A dialog is a single layer of focus; stacking another overlay (dialog, alert dialog, dropdown menu) inside it breaks focus containment and dismissal semantics. Astryx renders whatever it is passed (guidance-over-enforcement); this rule is the enforcement its docs' modality guidance implies.

"Table displays structured data in rows and columns with consistent dimensionality"; "Use Table for data sets with uniform structure; for simpler or inconsistent data, consider a list or card layout instead". A record collection is uniform by definition — same fields on every record — so it renders as a table, not as prose or ad-hoc stacks. (The catalog's list component serves heterogeneous and selectable content, per the same docs; uniform records still choose tables. A table-or-list choice set is inexpressible in current rule types — component-choice require is conjunctive — noted as candidate dspack-gen work if a scenario ever needs it.) — Astryx Table docs, v0.1.4

The catalog's Table is "DATA-DRIVEN: rows and columns are array props (`data`, `columns`), not sub-components" — a table without its `data` prop renders an empty shell, records nowhere to be seen. Born from observed live runs: gpt-oss reliably emitted the table shell and omitted the rows until this rule made the pipeline ask for them. — Astryx Table docs, v0.1.4

"TextInput collects short-form text like names, emails, or search queries"; Button "triggers an action when clicked. Use it for form submissions, confirmations, navigation, or any interaction that needs a clear call to action". An ask without a field is not a form, and a form that cannot be submitted does not ship: a data-collection surface carries at least one input and an explicit action. No layout, field count, or form shape is mandated. — Astryx TextInput and Button docs, v0.1.4

SelectableCard is for "plan pickers, filter chips, or option grids"; List renders "ordered or unordered groups of related content" — "lists imply a meaningful collection"; Button "triggers an action when clicked … any interaction that needs a clear call to action". A review surface presents its options as a collection of named selectable cards with an explicit committing action — not prose, not a bare recommendation. — Astryx SelectableCard, List, and Button docs, v0.1.4

A choice of one is not a comparison: a review's option list carries at least two selectable cards. (Under this intent, lists are option groups; the floor applies to every list on the surface. Exact counts and upper bounds are inexpressible in current rule types and remain guidance.)

"MetadataList displays key-value pairs for object attributes" — an option you can compare states what it offers as structured attributes, not free prose. Every selectable card under this intent carries a metadata list. — Astryx MetadataList docs, v0.1.4

The catalog's MetadataList is data-driven: attributes are `{ label, value }` records. A metadata list without its items renders an empty shell, attributes nowhere to be seen — the same class of failure rule.table-carries-data guards against for tables.
Every option is named: SelectableCard's label is how an alternative is identified, compared, and announced. An unnamed option cannot be chosen deliberately. — Astryx SelectableCard docs, v0.1.4

examples.name: Delete project confirmation
examples.prompt: a screen to delete a project
examples.description: A card presenting the destructive entry point (a labeled destructive button) alongside the AlertDialog carrying its required title, consequence description, and a specific action label — the Astryx props-based idiom (no trigger sub-component).

children.text: Deleting a project removes all of its data permanently.

props.title: Delete this project?
props.description: This action cannot be undone. The project and all its data will be permanently removed.

examples.name: Book a consultation
examples.prompt: a screen to book a consultation time
examples.description: A card collecting the booker's name (labeled, required) beside selectable time buttons and an explicit, specifically-labeled confirm action — time choice and confirmation on one surface.

children.text: Pick a time that works. Your name is required to hold the slot.

examples.name: Recipe creator
examples.prompt: an editable weeknight recipe with servings and dietary constraints
examples.description: A card presenting a co-edited structured object: a titled recipe with a dietary badge, labeled servings controls, an ingredients table, a labeled constraint input, a bound status line, and explicit edit/regenerate actions.

children.text: Edit servings or add a constraint.

examples.name: Support-ticket triage
examples.prompt: a triage view of open support tickets with status and priority
examples.description: A card leading with a badge strip summarizing queue status, above a compact table of the open tickets — the collection renders as a table; statuses that need attention are badged, the rest stay plain text.

children.text: Newest first. Statuses update as agents respond.

examples.name: Signup, name and email
examples.prompt: a signup form asking for name and email, with a clear call to action
examples.description: A card stating the ask, two labeled fields, and one explicit primary action — something to fill in and a way to send it, every field labeled.

children.text: Name and email is all it takes. You can add the rest later.

props.label: Email address
props.description: We send a confirmation link, nothing else.

examples.name: Plan comparison
examples.prompt: compare the available plans and choose one
examples.description: Two named plans as selectable cards in a list, each stating its attributes as a metadata list, with one explicit committing action. Deliberately not a hotel: the intent is domain-neutral, and the scenario that validates it comes from a different domain.

children.text: Two plans, compared on what they include. Pick one to continue.
